title: Lecture Hall Reset
desc: An academy hall in the Learning Quadrant still looks like yesterday's demonstration won the argument against order.

steps.text:
The Learning Quadrant always looks composed from the outside. Courtyards swept, banners aligned, windows bright, students pretending composure where possible. Inside the lower lecture hall, the illusion has lost ground. Yesterday's elemental demonstration left chalk across half the stone, a set of resonance boards leaning where they should not lean, and three sand trays in the kind of configuration that suggests either vigorous instruction or an avoidable disagreement.

The assistant instructor hands you a cloth, a brush, and a look that says she has already done the truly irritating part. "Reset the chalk slates and resonance boards if you want the wall work," she says. "Or lay out the sand trays and sample stands for the afternoon class if you prefer the floor. Both need doing before the hall starts pretending it is scholarly again."

options.label: Reset the chalk slates and resonance boards
Lay out the sand trays and sample stands for the next lesson

steps.text:
The slates are marked with the remains of yesterday's equations, half-erased diagrams, and one emphatic correction driven so hard into the chalk that the board still looks argumentative after cleaning. You wipe them down, square the frames, and hang the resonance boards back in proper progression so the largest plates sit where the next lesson can build from them without hunting around the room. Once the wall order returns, the whole hall settles with it.

The assistant instructor tests one board with a careful knuckle tap. The note rings true and clean instead of clattering off-angle through the room. She seems relieved to hear discipline restored to sound as well as sight. "Better," she says. "Now the students may once again confuse preparation with inevitability."

options.label: Stack the clean cloths and leave the boards ready

steps.text:
The afternoon lesson needs the room to make sense before anyone arrives certain that they already do. You level the sand trays along the front tier, refill the shallow sample dishes, and set the small mineral stands in the sequence the instructor prefers: harmless first, instructive second, embarrassing third. By the time you finish, the floor arrangement directs the eye exactly where it should go and denies the students any excuse to claim the demonstration tables were confusing.

From the upper seats, the hall looks balanced again. The trays sit even, the stands catch the light, and the central aisle is clear enough for actual movement rather than academic improvisation. The assistant instructor studies the layout from halfway up the stairs and approves with a single short nod. "Good. If they fail now, it will at least be an honest failure."

options.label: Step clear of the aisle and leave the hall prepared
